fact: August 1 is exactly 213 days into the year, which means there are exactly 152 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is typically peak butterfly season - monarchs are often at their most active and colorful right now!
The sun rises about 2 minutes later than it did just a week ago, even though summer feels like it's still going strong.
Cicadas in North America are usually screaming their loudest around August 1 - some can be heard from over a mile away!
This date falls in the middle of Leo season, when the constellation Leo is well-positioned in the night sky.
In Australia and New Zealand, August 1 marks the official first day of spring, even though it might still feel chilly!
The Perseid meteor shower typically begins its spectacular show in early August, ramping up around this time.
Tree sap flows the slowest on hot August days like this one, which is why maple syrup is never made in summer.
August 1 is one of only 7 dates in the year that falls on the same day of the week as New Year's Day in non-leap years.
Honeybees often collect their heaviest loads of nectar during the first week of August when wildflowers are typically at peak bloom.
Ocean temperatures in many places reach their warmest point of the year around August 1, even though the sun started getting weaker in June.
Grasshoppers and crickets are at their jumpiest right now - they can leap 20 times their own body length in the August heat!
